nativeRange.habitat: Pelagic-neustonic among drifting rafts of Sargassum seaweed and other floating debris, typically at the surface from 0 to ~2 m though documented down to ~50 m

diet.notes: Ambush predator that wiggles a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy esca to lure small fish, shrimp, and other crustaceans. Has been documented expanding its mouth via suction feeding to engulf prey equal to or larger than its own body length. New specimens generally accept only live feeder fish or shrimp, though many can be trained over time onto frozen krill, silversides, or pieces of marine fish offered from tongs. Feeding every 4–6 days is commonly advised — overfeeding has been reported to cause bloat and feeding refusal.

goodWith: larger peaceful fish too big to be swallowed
avoidWith: aggressive nippers that target the lure

summary: A small pelagic frogfish that lives among drifting Sargassum mats and lures prey with a modified dorsal-fin appendage. Cryptic ambush predator; will swallow tankmates equal to its own length.
careNotes:
The Sargassum Fish (Histrio histrio) is the only species in its genus and a true pelagic frogfish — unlike most Antennariidae, it lives at the surface among drifting rafts of Sargassum and other floating weed rather than on the reef floor. Its body is laterally compressed with leaf-like skin filaments and a mottled tan/brown/yellow/green coloration that closely mimics the seaweed it inhabits. Individuals can shift hue over time to match their immediate surroundings.

The fish hunts by wiggling a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy esca that mimics a small prey item; once a target approaches, the fish strikes with one of the fastest documented feeding actions in vertebrates, expanding its mouth many times its resting volume. It has been documented swallowing prey equal to or exceeding its own body length, and is reported to be cannibalistic toward conspecifics — solitary keeping is generally advised.

Sources disagreed on minimum tank size: LiveAquaria states 30 gallons (~114 L), Saltwaterfish.com 50 gallons (~190 L), AquariumDomain 55 gallons (~208 L), and Reefs4Less 60 gallons (~227 L). Following the AquaMate convention of biasing toward the larger value for ambush predators that demand swimming room and hunting structure, a 200-L minimum was chosen. Body length is modest (15–20 cm), but the fish appreciates open horizontal space for the lure-and-strike hunting cycle, and providing floating algae or sargassum-style perches near the surface mimics the natural niche.

Reef-safety is nuanced: the fish ignores corals and will not nip them, so it is generally considered reef-compatible in that narrow sense. However, it will eat any fish or motile invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp, small wrasses, gobies, blennies, and cardinalfish. It should NOT be housed with triggerfish or pufferfish, which have been reported to bite off the lure appendage.

A few sources disagreed widely on lifespan (Live Gem Factory cited up to 20 years; Saltwaterfish.com cited 3–5 years). The shorter range is more consistent with the species' small body size and high-metabolism predatory lifestyle, so the 3–5 year figure was used; the 20-year claim appears to be an outlier and may conflate this species with the much longer-lived large frogfishes (e.g., Antennarius commerson).

Feeding is the principal husbandry hurdle. Newly-acquired specimens commonly refuse anything but live prey, and starvation is a documented risk if a transition to frozen foods is not patiently established. Many keepers train the fish to take frozen silversides or krill from tongs by mimicking prey motion.

The species is known to leap above the surface onto floating weed mats to escape predators in the wild, and can survive briefly out of water; a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. No venomous spines are documented. FishBase notes that ciguatera poisoning has been reported from this species (relevant for edibility, not for aquarium handling).
breedingNotes: Oviparous. Females ascend toward the surface and release eggs bound together in a gelatinous mucus 'raft' or 'veil' that drifts among the sargassum until hatching; males fertilize the raft externally. Captive breeding has been recorded but is uncommon, and pair maintenance is complicated by reports of cannibalism toward conspecifics outside the brief spawning window.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry sheet — sizing (30 gal min, 6 in max), water parameters (72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), reef-compatible rating, carnivore diet, peaceful temperament rating, ambush-predator behavior.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Antennariidae, Lophiiformes), maximum length (20 cm TL), pelagic-neustonic habitat among floating Sargassum, global distribution (Atlantic + Indo-Pacific), depth range 0–50 m, preferred temperature 21.4–29.2 °C (mean 27.7 °C), oviparous reproduction with gelatinous egg raft, IUCN Least Concern. Replaces designated secondary (Reef2Reef thread returned 403).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Histrio histrio)
additional.notes: Cross-checked biology and behavior. Confirmed monotypic genus, max length 20 cm, lure morphology (illicium + esca), cannibalistic ambush predation with rapid suction strike, ability to leap above the surface onto weed mats and survive briefly out of water, gelatinous egg-raft reproduction.

additional.name: AquariumDomain (Sargassum Anglerfish)
additional.notes: Cross-checked hobbyist husbandry — 55 gal minimum, 72–80 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, semi-aggressive temperament, moderate care level, and the specific caution to avoid housing with puffers or triggers (which damage the lure appendage).

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com (Sargassum Angler)
additional.notes: Triangulated tank-size (50 gal / ~190 L), water parameters (74–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025), aggressive temperament rating, and 3–5 year lifespan estimate.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with a gelatinous floating egg raft. Captive breeding is uncommon; pair maintenance is difficult given reports of cannibalism toward conspecifics outside spawning.
